title: Chicken pot pie
subtitle: Creamy chicken and vegetable filling under a golden shortcrust lid
excerpt: A deep-dish pie of poached chicken, carrots, celery, peas, and onion in a rich cream sauce, topped with a shortcrust pastry lid and baked until golden. The filling is made first and cooled, then topped with pastry and baked. One of the most comforting things an oven can produce.
sourceNotes: Chicken pot pie appears in American domestic cookery from the early nineteenth century; Fannie Farmer's 'The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book' (1896, Project Gutenberg #7544) includes a chicken pie preparation. The covered pastry version with a cream-based sauce became the mid-twentieth-century American standard.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unbaked — assemble the pie completely but do not bake. Wrap tightly in foil and freeze for up to 3 months. Bake from frozen at 180°C fan for 55 to 60 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The filling can be made up to 2 days ahead and refrigerated. Top with pastry and bake just before serving.

content.text: The filling is a thick cream sauce (a velouté built on chicken stock and finished with cream), loaded with cooked chicken and vegetables. It needs to be thick enough to hold together under the pastry lid; a filling that is too loose will make the base soggy and the serving messy. Cook the sauce until it coats the back of a spoon heavily before adding the chicken and vegetables.

Use ready-made shortcrust pastry if you prefer — there is no shame in it for a weekday pot pie. For a homemade lid, a simple shortcrust (plain flour, cold butter, a little water) takes 10 minutes to make and rests in the fridge while the filling cools. The pastry must not touch hot filling or it will melt before baking. Cut a few steam vents in the lid before it goes into the oven.

items.prepNote: poached and roughly torn into pieces

content.text: Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a saucepan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}}, {{carrot}}, and {{celery}} and cook for 8 to 10 minutes until softened. Add the {{plain-flour}} of plain flour and stir for 1 minute to form a roux. Gradually pour in the {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock, whisking to prevent lumps, then add the {{double-cream}} of double cream. Bring to a gentle simmer and cook, stirring, for 5 minutes until thick. Add the chicken pieces and {{peas-frozen}} of frozen peas, stir to combine, and season generously with salt and pepper. Leave to cool to room temperature before topping with pastry.

Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Spoon the cooled filling into the pie dish. Roll the {{shortcrust-pastry}} to a thickness of 3 mm and lay it over the dish, pressing the edges against the rim and trimming the excess. Brush with beaten {{eggs}} egg wash. Cut 3 or 4 small slits in the centre for steam to escape. Bake for 30 to 35 minutes until the pastry is deep golden and the filling is bubbling at the edges. Rest for 10 minutes before serving.

items.symptom: Pastry lid sinks into the filling
items.cause: Filling too liquid and hot when the pastry was applied
items.fix: Cool the filling until it holds its shape before topping; a thick filling supports the pastry, a thin one does not

items.symptom: Pastry is pale and soft on the underside
items.cause: Filling moisture steaming up through the base of the lid
items.fix: Make sure the filling is thick enough; very liquid fillings will steam and prevent the pastry underside from crisping

items.symptom: Filling is too thin and runs out when served
items.cause: Sauce not reduced enough before adding chicken
items.fix: The sauce should coat the back of a spoon heavily before you add the chicken and vegetables

items.cause: Pastry too dry, or not thick enough when rolled
items.fix: Roll to 3 mm minimum and handle gently; cracks before baking can be sealed with a little water

content.text: Chicken pot pie belongs to the broad tradition of British and American savoury pies, carried into North America by English and Welsh settlers who brought the pie-making tradition with them. It became one of the canonical American comfort foods of the twentieth century, with frozen commercial versions available from the 1950s onward (Marie Callender and Swanson both made it a household name). The commercial versions are a shadow of the real thing, which involves an actual pastry crust and a filling with enough flavour to stand on its own. It is the kind of meal that rewards the time it takes to make.
